coredata.dc:title: DFT-D3 and TD-DFT studies on the sensor performance of the functionalized carbon quantum dots for curcumin
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Longyan
Li, Xinyu
Zhang, Yi
Yin, Pengfei
Sun, Xiyuan
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, the interaction of curcumin molecule with a series of functionalized carbon quantum dots (f-CQDs, f=-OH, -NH2, -COOH) was investigated for drug sensing purposes by using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. After functionalization, the adsorption capacity of CQDs for curcumin was improved to a certain extent. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) energy decomposition revealed that the dispersion force played a dominant role in the stabilizing. The energy gap of the CQDs was reduced through functionalization. The adsorption of curcumin led to a significant energy gap change especially for CQDs with -COOH functionalized (-21.39 %), suggesting its promising sensor performance to curcumin. The detection ability of CQDs and f-CQDs for curcumin was further assessed by simulating the ultra-violet visible (UV–vis) and infrared (IR) spectra.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
Carbon quantum dots
Curcumin
Interaction
Spectra

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Curcumin adsorption onto CQDs and f-CQDs
Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the funding support from the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC1824).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The diketone form of curcumin. (b) The enol tautomeric form of curcumin.
(a) The optimized structure of curcumin. (b) The MEP plot of curcumin. (c) HOMO profiles of curcumin. (d) LUMO profiles of curcumin.

(a1-a3) The optimized structure, HOMO and LUMO profiles of CQDs. (b1-b3) The optimized structure, HOMO and LUMO profiles of CQDs-OH. (c1-c3) The optimized structure, HOMO and LUMO profiles of CQDs-NH2. (d1-d3) The optimized structure, HOMO and LUMO profiles of CQDs-COOH. (isovalue = 0.04 a.u).
Optimized structures of curcumin@CQDs (a), curcumin@CQDs-OH (b), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (c) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (d).
The total density of states (DOS) and partial DOS of nucleobase in different complexes, curcumin@CQDs (A), curcumin@CQDs-OH (B), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (C) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (D).

Calculated molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces, CQDs(a), CQDs-OH (b), CQDs-NH2 (c), CQDs-COOH (d), curcumin@CQDs(e), curcumin@CQDs-OH (f), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (g) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (h).
Molecular graph of curcumin@CQDs (a), curcumin@CQDs-OH (b), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (c)and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (d); the lines are bond paths.
Graphical representation of SAPT analysis, curcumin@CQDs (a), curcumin@CQDs-OH (b), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (c) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (d).
IR spectra, curcumin@CQDs (Complex A), curcumin@CQDs-OH (Complex B), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (Complex C) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (Complex D).
UV–visible spectra, curcumin@CQDs (Complex A), curcumin@CQDs-OH (Complex B), curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (Complex C) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (Complex D).

The adsorption energy (Eads), shortest adsorption distance, and charge transfers (CT) of curcumin molecule on the CQDs/f-CQDs, HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (Eg), and change in HOMO-LUMO gap of CQDs/f-CQDs upon the adsorption process. (A, B, C, and D are curcumin@CQDs, curcumin@CQDs-OH, curcumin@CQDs-NH2, and curcumin@CQDs-COOH complexes, respectively).
SAPT analysis of complexes and the unit of energy is kcal mol−1.

head.ce:title: DFT-D3 and TD-DFT studies on the sensor performance of the functionalized carbon quantum dots for curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'an 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'an 625014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, the interaction of curcumin molecule with a series of functionalized carbon quantum dots (f-CQDs, f=-OH, -NH2, -COOH) was investigated for drug sensing purposes by using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. After functionalization, the adsorption capacity of CQDs for curcumin was improved to a certain extent. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) energy decomposition revealed that the dispersion force played a dominant role in the stabilizing. The energy gap of the CQDs was reduced through functionalization. The adsorption of curcumin led to a significant energy gap change especially for CQDs with -COOH functionalized (-21.39 %), suggesting its promising sensor performance to curcumin. The detection ability of CQDs and f-CQDs for curcumin was further assessed by simulating the ultra-violet visible (UV–vis) and infrared (IR) spectra.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin [1,7-bis (4‑hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl) −1,6-heptadiene-3,5‑dione] (see Fig. 1 ) is a polyphenol extracted from turmeric (Curcuma longa L.), which is often used as a food flavoring agent and coloring agent. It has been widely recognized for its strong antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antiviral, and anticancer activities [1]. In recent years, curcumin has made significant advances as a bioactive substance in malignant tumors, Alzheimer's disease (AD), liver diseases, hematological disorders, dermatological disorders, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal diseases [2–6]. The curcumin molecule in the crystal structure exists in enol form, and according to the calculation (B3LYP/6–311G*) the enol form is more stable than diketo [7]. (see Fig. 1) The enolic form of curcumin can readily chelate the metal ions to form the complexes also scavenge the active free radicals [8]. However, excess curcumin has a promoting effect on the oxidative activity of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) [9] and can leads to a decrease in intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) levels and trigger necrotic processes [10]. Therefore, it is important to develop simple and rapid methods for the determination of curcumin content.

In 2020, Yao et al. proposed a fluorescent assay for the detection of curcuminoids using polyvinylpyrrolidone templated Cu NCs (PVP-Cu NCs) as a fluorescent probe [11]. Silva-Buzanello et al. used a UV–Vis based spectroscopic analysis to quantify curcumin encapsulated in poly (l-lactic acid) nanoparticles [12]. Chromatography, fluorescence and spectrophotometry are usually time-consuming, expensive, complicated sample pretreatment, and cumbersome procedures, which limit the low-cost and rapid detection of curcumin. Nanostructure-based electrochemical biosensors provided a solution to compensate for these drawbacks with the detection advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, small size, simple operation, fast response, and remarkable selectivity for the detection of a variety of substances at low concentrations [13–15]. Nanomaterials have received attention due to their unique physicochemical properties such as interfacial effects, high specific surface area, chemical activity, and enhanced diffusivity [16–18]. Various nanomaterials including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, nanoclusters, nanosheets, graphene, and quantum dots have been widely utilized in electrochemical biosensors [19–24].

As an emerging material in the field of nanomaterials, quantum dots have attracted much attention due to their unique tunable properties since they were first synthesized in 2004 [25]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an important branch of quantum dots, have attracted great attention in the field of analytical testing. CQDs are novel carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm, which have the advantages of low cost, photostability, good water solubility, low toxicity, easy preparation, and good biocompatibility [26]. Therefore, CQDs have a promising future in several applications such as drug delivery [27,28], catalysis [29], bioimaging [30], biomedicine [31] and sensors [32], etc. CQDs are ideal for the fabrication of fluorescent probes and ultra-small electrochemical biosensors due to their unique optical properties and the high stability of atom adsorption on the surface.

Surface functionalization of CQDs had a high impact as it helps to improve the performance of carbon nanoparticles [33]. There were a large number of studies showing that CQDs containing hydroxyl, amino or carboxyl groups had excellent surface functionalization and hydrophilicity, which had a wide range of applications in electrochemical biosensors. Recently, Thulasinathan et al. synthesized functionalized amino containing CQDs as electrochemical biosensors to measure pyocyanin (PYO) in actual urine samples, and have become potential candidates for real-time POC diagnosis of pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in clinical samples [34]. Korah et al. synthesized CQDs with hydroxyl and carboxyl group modifications using leaves as biomass carbon source, and the functionalized CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity as electrochemical biosensors for the detection of Cu2+ [35]. John et al. synthesized surface-rich hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups as electrochemical sensors for tetracycline (TC) by hydrothermal method, and the group doping provided CQDs with excellent stability and sensing ability [36]. Currently, research on experimental new approaches to sensors modified with CQDs continues, but theoretical studies are scarce.

In this work, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were applied to probe the interaction of prinstine/functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs/f-CQDs) with curcumin molecule for drug sensing. The adsorption energy, energy gaps between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and charge transfer were calculated for the stabilized model system and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) were utilized to characterize the interactions and bonding properties of curcumin with CQDs/f-CQDs. Finally, they were further investigated by infrared (IR) and ultra-violet visible (UV–Vis) spectra.

The initial adsorption structures of curcumin on CQDs and f-CQDs (f = -OH, -NH2, -COOH) were obtained with the artificial bee colony algorithm of Karaboga [37] as implemented in the ABCluster 2.0 program [38]. In many previous works [39–41], ABCluster has been used to generate cluster structures. Furthermore, it has been shown that ABCluster was more efficient compared to other structural prediction software [42]. Several low-lying structures in ABCluster runs were further optimized by using DFT calculations with the ORCA program [43]. All calculations were performed with the PBE [44] functional in conjunction with the def2-SVP basis set, and the DFT-D3 correction proposed by Grimme [45] was adopted to describe van der Waals (vdW) dispersion interaction. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analyses were performed through the Multiwfn program [46]. To assess the binding ability of curcumin molecule with CQDs and f-CQDs, we defined the adsorption energy (Eads) of the complexes in the usual way as follows [47]:
(1)
E
a d s
=
E
c o m p l e x
−
(
E
C Q D s / f − C Q D s
+
E
c u r c u m i n
)
where Ecomplex is the total energy of the CQDs/f-CQDs@curcumin complexes, and Ecurcumin and ECQDs/f-CQDs are the energies of the individual curcumin and CQDs/f-CQDs, respectively. The basis set superposition errors (BSSE) were corrected in the calculations of adsorption energies [48]. The energy decomposition calculations were performed with SAPT [49] as implemented in the Psi4 program [50]. The interaction energies were calculated at the SAPT0/aug-cc-pVDZ level of theory.

Frequency calculations were performed to assess the kinetic stability of the complexes studied and to predict the infrared (IR) spectra. The optical absorption properties and electronic transition properties of CQDs and f-CQDs with curcumin adsorption were investigated through the time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations at the PBE0-D3 level of theory. The computed electronic absorption spectra were broadened by the Gaussian function with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 0.3 eV with the Multiwfn software [46].

The more stable enolic structure of curcumin was considered in the present study [7]. The optimized structure of the curcumin molecule, molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) plot, and its isosurfaces of the frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) are shown in Fig. 2 . The optimized C1-O1, C2-O2 distances of 1.32 Å and 1.28 Å were close to the 1.33 Å and 1.25 Å, respectively reported by Kolev et al. [7]. The MEP of the curcumin shown in Fig. 2b indicates that the oxygen atoms in the enol (-C = COH) group and carbonyl group (-C = O) have the highest negative electrostatic potential and the largest electrophilic tendency. The benzene ring, some of the carbon atoms in the middle, and the oxygen atoms on the phenolic hydroxyl group have a slightly negative electrostatic potential. These negatively charged atoms can be adsorbed on the electron-withdrawing portions of CQDs and f-CQDs. As it shown in Fig. 2c and d, the HOMO of curcumin shows a π character which is mainly from the carbon atoms, and LUMO is mainly from the contributions of the p orbital of partial carbon and oxygen atoms in the middle.

The molecular structure of CQDs used in our study is a carbon cluster model (C54H18), which has been successfully employed in recent works [51–53]. The relaxed structure of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3 (a1), and the calculated CC bond lengths in the range of 1.42–1.44 Å are comparable to those observed in the experiments [54]. Six hydrogen atoms at the edges of the CQDs were replaced by hydroxyl (-OH), amino (-NH2), and carboxyl (-COOH) groups, respectively, to construct the f-CQDs. After optimization and frequency calculations, the stable structures are obtained as shown in Fig. 3 (b1–d1). The length of the CO bond in the CQDs-OH structure is 1.36 Å, the length of the CN bond in the CQDs-NH2 structure is 1.39 Å, and the lengths of the CO and C = O bonds in the CQDs-COOH structure are 1.36 Å and 1.22 Å, respectively.

The FMO of CQDs and f-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 (a2–d3), and the corresponding energies and HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (Eg) are listed in Table 1 . All species of HOMO and LUMO are mainly contributed by the surrounding carbon atoms, showing distinct π character and P-orbital contributions, respectively. After careful observation, one can find that the p orbital of N or O of the functional group has a small contribution to the FMO. As can be seen from Table 1, the relative magnitudes of Eg for CQDs and f-CQDs are in the order of CQDs > CQDs-OH > CQDs-COOH > CQDs-NH2. It is important to note that the functionalization for CQDs leads to the decrease of HOMO-LUMO energy gaps. The Eg changes magnitude may be related to the electronegativity of groups (-NH2 > -COOH > -OH). For example, for CQDs-NH2 which has the smallest Eg value, direct doping of -NH2 can resonate with the effective orbitals of CQDs due to the fact that the nitrogen in -NH2 adjacent to the carbon atom has a larger electronegativity than that of carbon, which induces an upward shift of the Fermi energy level, resulting in an increase in the electronic activity of the conduction band and a decrease in the Eg. It can be concluded from the above that f-CQDs narrow the Eg, and the Eg of CQDs can be regulated by changing the electronic energy levels of the edge states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curcumin adsorption onto CQDs and f-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ABCluster program was used to screen out the adsorption structures of curcumin molecule on CQDs and f-CQDs, and further geometry optimizations were carried out with ORCA to find the optimal adsorption position. The most stable adsorption configurations of curcumin on the surface of CQDs and f-CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . Table 1 summarizes the adsorption energy (Eads), shortest adsorption distance (dmin), charge transfers (CT), HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (Eg), and change in HOMO-LUMO gap of CQDs/f-CQDs upon the adsorption process. The optimum adsorption position of CQDs and f-CQDs with curcumin is parallel adsorption. For the most stable complex of curcumin@CQDs (complex A), the shortest adsorption distance is 3.21 Å related to the OC bond. The Eads value is −0.95 eV with BSSE correction, which suggests the reaction is exothermic. The Eg value of complex A is 1.72 eV, which is decreased compared to CQDs (1.88 eV). The Mulliken population shows that the charge is transferred from the curcumin molecule to CQDs with a magnitude of 0.036 (e).

As shown in Fig. 4, it can be observed that curcumin is attached by CQD-NH2 through a CC bond with the shortest adsorption distance of 3.24 Å. The adsorption of curcumin on both CQDs-OH and CQDs-COOH was through OO bond with the shortest adsorption distances of 3.02 Å and 3.01 Å, respectively, and the equilibrium adsorption distances in curcumin@CQDs-OH (complex B) and curcumin@CQDs-COOH (complex D) were the shorter than those in curcumin@CQDs (complex A) and curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (complex C). One can see from Table 1 that the values of Eads for f-CQDs ranging from −1.15 to −1.25 eV are in the order of complex D > complex C > complex B. The Eads values are all negative, suggesting that the reaction can proceed spontaneously. Besides the Eads values of curcumin molecule with f-CQDs were higher than that of CQDs, indicating that the functionalization of CQDs with -OH, -NH2, and -COOH groups on the edge can enhance the adsorption capacity for curcumin molecule. We also compared the adsorption strength of curcumin on other nanomaterials [55–57]. The Eads of curcumin on CQDs and f-CQDs were greater than those of other nanomaterials, indicating that the adsorption of curcumin on CQDs and f-CQDs was more stable. The charge transfer (CT) values of complexes B, C, and D are 0.022, −0.023, and 0.027 (e), respectively, suggesting that curcumin is the electron acceptor in the structure of complex C and electron donor in the structures of complexes B and D. In addition, the CT of all complexes studied are not significant, and we hypothesize that there may be non-covalent interactions between curcumin and CQDs and f-CQDs.

The electronic properties of CQDs and f-CQDs before and after curcumin adsorption were investigated by partial density of states (PDOS). The results are shown in Fig. 5 , one can observe that no significant orbital overlaps between the curcumin molecule and CQDs/f-CQDs, indicating a weak interaction between them. Moreover, the curcumin molecule makes significant contributions to the density of states near the Fermi energy level, which is also confirmed by the FMO isosurfaces (see Fig. S1 from supporting information). This indicates that the adsorption of curcumin molecule onto CQDs and f-CQDs leads to the electronic structure change. The adsorption complexes (A, B, C, and D) show narrow energy gaps relative to free CQDs/f-CQDs, which further accelerates the process of charge transfer.

The Eg parameter indicates the sensitivity and reactivity of the adsorbent to the adsorbate. This adsorption is expected to cause significant changes in conductivity [58]:
(2)
σ ∝ e x p
(
−
E g
2 K T
)
where σ is the electrical conductivity and K is the Boltzmann constant. According to Eq. (2), when the value of Eg changes it changes σ, σ becomes an electrical signal.

The magnitude of ΔEg% values (see Table 1) indicate the strength of the electrical signals, confirming the presence of curcumin. The percentages of ΔEg were 8.51 %, 8.74 %, 11.11 %, and 21.39 % for A, B, C, and D complexes, respectively. It can be seen that the Eg values of CQDs-COOH changed significantly after the adsorption of curcumin, and the sensing efficiency of CQDs-COOH for curcumin was fairly high. We can conclude that the molecular sensing performance of CQDs and f-CQDs for curcumin is, in descending order, CQDs-COOH>CQDs-NH2>CQDs-OH>CQDs.

According to the MEP isosurface plot shown in Fig. 6 , from blue to red color represents the change of electrostatic potential from negative to positive values, with the blue region susceptible to attack by electrophilic reagents and the red region susceptible to attack by nucleophilic reagents. From the MEP surface of Fig. 6 (a–d), it can be seen that the carbon backbone on the surface of CQDs-NH2 presents more negative values compared to CQDs, CQDs-OH, and CQDs-COOH. The MEP value of CQDs-NH2 predicts that the binding energy of CQDs-NH2 complexes having carbon is greater than that of those presenting a positive electrostatic potential amine head, acting as an electron donor through the carbon atom. Therefore, this result suggests a charge transfer from curcumin to CQDs-NH2, which can be confirmed by the charge transfer results. From the MEP plots of Fig. 6 (e–h), we can see that after binding of curcumin to CQDs and f-CQDs, the color (blue) of the electrostatic potential isosurfaces of complexes A, B, and D in the binding region becomes darker compared to those of free CQDs and f-CQDs, whereas the color (blue) of the electrostatic potential isosurfaces of complex C is significantly lighter compared to that of curcumin (see Fig. 2b). It indicates that curcumin has a charge transfer effect with CQDs and f-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: QTAIM has been widely used to analyze binding interaction characters. The interactions between CQDs/f-CQDs with curcumin are topologically analyzed as shown in Fig. 7 . In these four complexes, there are topological paths between C, H, O, and N atoms on the surface of CQDs/f-CQDs and C, H, and O atoms of the curcumin molecule, and the bond critical points (BCPs) related to these atoms are formed in the paths.

The charge density ρ(r), Laplacian of charge density (∇2ρ), potential energy density V(r), kinetic energy density G(r), and energy density H(r) in BCPs are extracted to determine the nature of the interaction. The topological parameters of the BCPs are collected in Table S1 (in supporting information), and the Laplacian values of ∇2ρ for the electron densities at each BCP of complexes A, B, C, D are all positive, indicating that the interactions corresponding to these BCPs are closed-shell interactions [59]. According to the theory proposed by Cremer [60]: |V(r)|<G(r) and H(r)>0, indicating that the type of interaction is a non-covalent bonding interaction. The ρ(r) at the BCP is related to the strength of the interaction. The higher its value, the stronger the interaction. As can be seen from Table S1, the values of both complex A and complex D at the BCPs are consistent with this theory, and we can speculate that the interactions of complex A and complex D are non-covalent and that the interaction of complex D is stronger than that of complex A. Espinosa et al. [61] suggested that if ∇2ρ > 0 and H(r) < 0, then transition closed-shell layer interactions are formed, the nature of bonding is intermediate between closed-shell layer interactions and covalent bonding. We also note that the OH bonds in complex B and the HN, OH bonds in complex C have H(r) < 0, which suggests that their interactions are partially covalent. However, they have less negative H(r), indicating that this interaction works more weakly. The other bonds in complexes B and C have positive ∇2ρ, H(r) and |V(r)|<G(r), indicating that their interactions are types of non-covalent interactions. In the case of non-covalent interactions, ρ values greater than 0.1 au indicates the presence of strong interactions such as hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions [62]. Similarly, ρ values less than 0.1 au indicate weak van der Waals interactions [63]. From Table S1, it can be seen that both complexes A and D have ρ(r) less than 0.1 au, and most of the BCPs in complexes B and C have ρ(r) less than 0.1 au, thus they belong to weak closed-shell interactions.

SAPT is used to account for the contribution of different non-covalent components. The interaction energy of SAPT is represented by the sum of electrostatic (Eest), exchange (Eexch), induction (Eind) and dispersion (Edis) energies. The SAPT results listed in Table 2 shows that most of the components have negative ESAPT energies, indicating an attractive contribution to stabilizing [64]. The interaction energies (ESAPT) of complexes A, B, C and D are −37.69, −44.91, −45.80 and −49.54 kcal mol−1, respectively, in an order of complex D > complex C > complex B > complex A, which is proportional to the magnitude of adsorption energy. Among the negative contributions, the electrostatic and dispersion components contribute more to the molecular stability [65] as shown in Fig. 8 , and the dispersive energy shares in complexes A, B, C and D are 72.77 %, 65.65 %, 67.32 % and 65.93 %, respectively, indicating that the dispersion component plays a predominated role in stabilizing the complexes. This is in agreement with the conclusion of van der Waals forces for noncovalent interactions analyzed by QTAIM results.

The IR spectra were simulated by frequency calculations. Fig. 9 shows the infrared spectra of the studied CQDs, f-CQDs, curcumin and complexes A, B, C and D over the entire spectral range. The peaks labeled in Fig. 9 are the important peaks discussed herein. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the peak at 1527.2 cm−1 of curcumin corresponds to the mixed C = O and C = C stretching vibration, and the predicted peak in the 1635.2 cm−1 band is related to the C = C antisymmetric stretching vibration. They have a new selection in complex A, redshifted to 1524.5 and 1624.5 cm−1, respectively. In addition to this, the phenol-OH stretching mode of curcumin is located at 3598.5 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of CQDs are mainly at 1632.5 and 3122.4 cm−1 which correspond to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of C = C and the symmetric stretching vibration of CH, respectively. For complex A, the vibrational mode of CH is located at 3115.7 cm−1, which is shifted compared to CQDs.

From Fig. 9(b), it can be obtained the molecular vibrational information of CQDs-OH and complex B. The absorption peaks of CQDs-OH at 1155.0 and 1635.2 cm−1 are the rocking bending vibration of -C-OH and the symmetric stretching vibration of C = O, and the absorption peak at 3703.9 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of -OH. Complex B has two -OH vibrational peaks at 3578.5 and 3699.9 cm−1, with wave numbers redshifted by 20 and 4 cm−1 compared to CQDs and curcumin, respectively. The absorption peak at 1635.2 cm−1 corresponds to the antisymmetric telescoping vibration of C = C. The absorption peak at 1523.2 cm−1 is red-shifted compared to that of curcumin at 1527.2 cm−1, again related to the mixed stretching vibrations of C = O and C = C.

As can be seen from Fig. 9(c), the absorption peak of CQDs-NH2 at 544.2 cm−1 is generated by the HN-H rocking bending vibration, and 1568.5 cm−1 and 1636.5 cm−1 are generated by the mixed C = C and CN telescoping vibration. The characteristic peaks in the 1568.5cm−1 and 1636.5cm−1 bands are red-shifted to the 1564.5 and 1635.2cm−1 bands of complex C with enhanced peak intensity, and the characteristic peak in the 544.2cm−1 band is blueshifted to 549.5cm−1 of complex C. The absorption peaks of curcumin in the 1527.2 and 3598.5 cm−1 bands are redshifted toward the lower bands of complex C at 1524.5 cm−1 (mixed telescopic vibrations of C = O and C = C) and 3589.2 cm−1 (phenol-OH stretching).

As shown in Fig. 9(d), the characteristic peak of CQDs-COOH at 1139.0 cm−1 is mainly generated by the COH symmetric stretching vibration of -COO, the absorption peak at 1756.6 cm−1 is mainly the C = O stretching vibration of -COOH group, and the absorption peak at 3642.5 cm−1 is the -OH stretching vibration. They are blueshifted to the complex D in the 1140.4 cm−1 band and redshifted to the 1755.2 and 3637.2 cm−1 bands, respectively. The absorption peak of curcumin at the 1527.2 cm−1 band corresponds to the same type of vibrational peaks at 1524.5 cm−1 of complex D and the redshifted by 2.7 cm−1. Complex D corresponds to the -C-O stretching vibration at 1335.1 cm−1. It can be seen that when CQDs and f-CQDs interact with curcumin, the vibration of the moiety is affected, resulting in a displacement of the infrared absorption peaks.

The UV-visible spectra of CQDs, f-CQDs and complexes studied were investigated using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations at the PBE0-D3 level of theory, considering 60 excited states. The key excited states, wavelengths (λ), oscillator strengths (f0) and dominant orbital contributions are given in Table S2 (in supporting information), and Fig. S2 (in supporting information) shows the corresponding molecular orbital isosurfaces. The simulated UV–vis spectra of the free monomers and complexes are shown in Fig. 10 . It has been reported that the main absorption peak of curcumin standard is around 422.40 nm [66], which is similar to the main absorption peak of curcumin 418.44 nm in our work. Both CQDs and f-CQDs have two adsorption peaks. The two main peaks of CQDs are at 271.00 and 422.29 nm (see Table S2). The two main peaks of f-CQDs are red-shifted to some degree compared to those of CQDs in particular for CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-COOH. In addition to this, it can be seen from Fig. S2 that the CQDs and f-CQDs orbitals mainly exhibit π characters and are concentrated on the carbon atom. As can be seen from Fig. 10(a), for complex A, the first peak is at 294.99 nm, which is red-shifted by 23.99 nm compared with that of CQDs at 271.00 nm, and the intensity of this absorption is reduced. The absorption peak at 294.99 nm is from S0→S56 excitation corresponding to the transition from HOMO-12 to LUMO+1. HOMO-12 and LUMO+1 are distributed in the π-bond and P-orbitals of CQD, respectively (e.g., Fig. S2). The second peak located at 413.28 nm corresponds to S0→S9 from HOMO-3 to LUMO transition, and this absorption peak is blue-shifted compared to that of the CQDs at 422.9 nm. The first absorption peak of curcumin@CQDs-OH (complex B) at 315.64 nm is red-shifted relative to CQDs-OH. It corresponds to the S0→S42 excitation mainly from HOMO-1 to LUMO+10 transition. HOMO-1 is predominantly the π bonds of CQDs-OH and LUMO+10 is predominantly the P-orbitals of CQDs-OH. The second distinct peak at 413.97 nm, which corresponds to S0→S9 excitation mainly attributed to HOMO-2 to LUMO+2 transition is blue-shifted by 18.48 nm compared to CQDs-OH. HOMO-2 is mainly concentrated in the π bonds of the curcumin molecule, and LUMO+2 is concentrated in the π bonds of curcumin and in the σ bonds of CQDs-OH. Also from Fig. 10, it can be concluded that the absorption peaks of curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (complex C) at 323.13 nm (S0→S45 from HOMO-8 to LUMO+2) and 424.31 nm (S0→S9 from HOMO-4 to LUMO+1) are red-shifted and blue-shifted by 33.79 and 32.35 nm, respectively, compared to free CQDs-NH2. HOMO-8 and HOMO-4 are mainly π characters of CQDs-NH2. LUMO+2 is contributed in the π-bonds of curcumin and in the σ bonds of CQDs-NH2. LUMO+1 is mainly contributed by the P orbital of CQDs-COOH. As for curcumin@CQDs-COOH (complex D), two distinct peaks appear at 317.99 and 428.86 nm, which is red-shifted and blue-shifted by 30.72 and 28.48 nm, respectively, relative to the free CQDs-COOH. These two peaks correspond to S0→S54 and S0→S10 excitations, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, HOMO-6 and HOMO-2 are mainly contributed by the σ bonds of CQDs-COOH, and LUMO+2 is mainly a π character of curcumin.

Based on the above analysis, it can be concluded that the adsorption of the curcumin molecule causes significant shifts of adsorption peaks in electronic spectra especially for CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-COOH. Our results indicate that the remarkable spectral shifts can help us to assess the sensor performance of f-CQDs for curcumin through experimental spectral techniques.

The interactions of curcumin with pristine and functionalized CQDs with -OH, -NH2, and -COOH were investigated by using density functional theory calculations. Our results show that curcumin is attached by CQDs and f-CQDs by parallel adsorption model. The values of adsorption energies indicated that curcumin adsorbs more strongly on f-CQDs, reaching −1.25 eV for CQDs-COOH. The value of HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of CQDs was to some degree decreased by functionalization. After adsorption of the curcumin molecule, the ΔEg% values of CQDs and f-CQDs were changed in particular for CQDs-COOH (21.39 %), indicating a significant increase in conductivity after adsorption. The MEP analysis showed that curcumin has charge transfer interactions with CQDs and f-CQDs. The results of QTAIM and SAPT revealed that the OH bonding H(r) < 0 in complex B, and the HN and OH bonding H(r) < 0 in complex C. Their interactions are intermediate between closed-shell interactions and covalent bonds. The dispersion force played a predominant role in the stabilization between curcumin and CQDs/f-CQDs. In addition, we simulated IR and UV–Vis spectra, and the results showed that the peak positions of the complexes are either blue-shifted or red-shifted to some extent after curcumin adsorption. The spectral shifts in CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-COOH are more significant than others. The sensing performance of the CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-COOH is more efficient in the detection of curcumin. Finally, our theoretical results illustrate that the electrochemical sensing performance of CQDs for curcumin were enhanced through modification, which is consistent with the current experimental study.

Longyan Yang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinyu Li: Data curation, Investigation. Yi Zhang: Investigation. Pengfei Yin: Methodology, Visualization. Xiyuan Sun: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding support from the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC1824).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.103870.
